examples.title: Basic Class Definition and Object Creation
examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental class syntax, __init__ constructor, instance attributes, and creating objects in Python
examples.code:
# Basic Class Definition
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, roll_no, marks):
        # Instance attributes
        self.name = name
        self.roll_no = roll_no
        self.marks = marks
    
    def display(self):
        print(f"Name: {self.name}")
        print(f"Roll No: {self.roll_no}")
        print(f"Marks: {self.marks}")
    
    def calculate_grade(self):
        if self.marks >= 90:
            return 'A'
        elif self.marks >= 80:
            return 'B'
        elif self.marks >= 70:
            return 'C'
        elif self.marks >= 60:
            return 'D'
        else:
            return 'F'

# Creating objects (instances)
student1 = Student("Amit Kumar", 101, 85)
student2 = Student("Priya Singh", 102, 92)

# Calling methods
print("Student 1 Details:")
student1.display()
print(f"Grade: {student1.calculate_grade()}")

print("\nStudent 2 Details:")
student2.display()
print(f"Grade: {student2.calculate_grade()}")
examples.explanation: The Student class is defined with an __init__ method (constructor) that initializes instance attributes (self.name, self.roll_no, self.marks). The self parameter refers to the current instance. The display() method prints student information, and calculate_grade() returns a grade based on marks using conditional statements. Objects student1 and student2 are created by passing arguments to the constructor.

examples.title: Encapsulation with Private Attributes and Property Decorators
examples.description: Demonstrates encapsulation principles using private variables, getters, setters, and property decorators for data hiding
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates encapsulation in Python using name mangling (double underscore __balance makes it private). The @property decorator creates getter methods, while @balance.setter allows controlled modification with validation. The __balance and __transaction_count are private attributes that cannot be accessed directly from outside the class, enforcing data hiding. Methods deposit() and withdraw() modify the private balance with validation logic.

examples.title: Inheritance, Method Overriding, and Polymorphism
examples.description: Demonstrates class inheritance, parent-child relationships, method overriding, super() function, and runtime polymorphism
examples.code:
# Base Class (Parent)
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age, email):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
        self.email = email
    
    def display_info(self):
        return f"Name: {self.name}, Age: {self.age}, Email: {self.email}"
    
    def calculate_retirement_age(self):
        return 60 - self.age

# Derived Class (Child) - Inheritance
class Student(Person):
    def __init__(self, name, age, email, student_id, course):
        super().__init__(name, age, email)  # Call parent constructor
        self.student_id = student_id
        self.course = course
        self.grades = {}
    
    def add_grade(self, subject, grade):
        self.grades[subject] = grade
    
    def calculate_gpa(self):
        if not self.grades:
            return 0
        return sum(self.grades.values()) / len(self.grades)
    
    # Method Overriding
    def display_info(self):
        base_info = super().display_info()
        return f"{base_info}, Student ID: {self.student_id}, Course: {self.course}"

# Another Derived Class
class Teacher(Person):
    def __init__(self, name, age, email, employee_id, subject, salary):
        super().__init__(name, age, email)
        self.employee_id = employee_id
        self.subject = subject
        self.salary = salary
    
    def display_info(self):
        base_info = super().display_info()
        return f"{base_info}, Employee ID: {self.employee_id}, Subject: {self.subject}, Salary: {self.salary}"
    
    def calculate_retirement_age(self):  # Method Overriding
        return 65 - self.age

# Demonstrate Polymorphism with function
def print_person_info(person):
    print(person.display_info())
    print(f"Years to retirement: {person.calculate_retirement_age()}")

# Create instances
student = Student("Dev Sharma", 20, "dev@email.com", "STU001", "BSc CS")
teacher = Teacher("Dr. Meera Joshi", 45, "meera@email.com", "EMP101", "Python Programming", 75000)

student.add_grade("Python", 85)
student.add_grade("Data Structures", 90)

# Polymorphism in action
print("=== Student Info ===")
print_person_info(student)
print(f"GPA: {student.calculate_gpa():.2f}")

print("\n=== Teacher Info ===")
print_person_info(teacher)

# Check inheritance relationship
print(f"\nIs student a Person? {isinstance(student, Person)}")
print(f"Is teacher a Student? {isinstance(teacher, Student)}")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates inheritance where Student and Teacher classes inherit from the Person base class using the syntax class Student(Person). The super().__init__() calls the parent constructor. Method overriding occurs when Student and Teacher redefine calculate_retirement_age() and display_info(). Polymorphism is shown through the print_person_info() function that accepts any Person object (or its subclasses). isinstance() demonstrates the inheritance relationship. This exemplifies core OOP principles required for the Delhi University B.Sc. Computer Science curriculum.
